explanation.summary: The nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) is the central processing center for baroreceptor afferents in the medulla. Damage to the NTS eliminates the brain's ability to process baroreceptor input and generate appropriate autonomic responses, resulting in labile blood pressure with wide fluctuations.
explanation.whyCorrect: The NTS receives afferent signals from baroreceptors via CN IX and X and integrates this information to modulate sympathetic and parasympathetic outflow. Damage to the NTS disrupts this central processing, rendering the baroreceptor reflex ineffective. Without beat-to-beat autonomic regulation, blood pressure becomes labile with wide swings because the body cannot compensate rapidly for normal physiological perturbations (standing, exertion, etc.).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Loss of NTS function would tend to cause hypertension and tachycardia (not hypotension and bradycardia) because the baroreceptor-mediated inhibition of sympathetic outflow is lost, allowing unopposed sympathetic activity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NTS damage affects all baroreceptor-mediated cardiovascular regulation, including heart rate, contractility, and vascular resistance. It does not selectively impair only contractility regulation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While there would be a tendency toward hypertension and tachycardia from unopposed sympathetic activity, the most characteristic finding is labile blood pressure because all baroreceptor-mediated regulation is disrupted, not just parasympathetic output.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While kidneys do regulate long-term blood pressure, they cannot provide the rapid second-to-second regulation needed for daily activities. Without baroreceptor regulation, significant blood pressure instability occurs.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is receiving a rapid intravenous infusion of normal saline during surgery. His right atrial pressure increases from 5 mm Hg to 15 mm Hg. Which of the following reflexes is most likely activated by this volume-induced atrial distension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carotid sinus baroreceptor reflex causing bradycardia and vasodilation to reduce arterial pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bezold-Jarisch reflex causing bradycardia, hypotension, and coronary vasodilation from ventricular mechanoreceptor activation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bainbridge reflex causing tachycardia to increase cardiac output and relieve the right atrial volume overload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cushing reflex causing hypertension and bradycardia from increased intracranial pressure compressing the brainstem

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic arch baroreceptor reflex causing decreased sympathetic output and reduced systemic vascular resistance

explanation.summary: The Bainbridge reflex is triggered by stretching of the right atrium due to increased venous return/volume overload. It causes tachycardia by inhibiting parasympathetic outflow, increasing cardiac output to relieve the volume overload in the right atrium.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Bainbridge reflex is the venous-side counterpart to the arterial baroreceptor reflex. When the right atrium is stretched by volume overload (as from rapid IV fluid infusion), receptors in the atrial wall send signals that inhibit parasympathetic outflow to the heart, resulting in tachycardia. This increased heart rate raises cardiac output, helping to move the excess blood through the heart and relieve the atrial distension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The carotid sinus baroreceptors detect changes in arterial pressure, not right atrial pressure. While the increased volume may eventually raise arterial pressure and activate this reflex, the primary reflex activated by right atrial distension is the Bainbridge reflex.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The Bezold-Jarisch reflex involves ventricular mechanoreceptors and causes bradycardia and hypotension. It is typically triggered by ventricular underfilling or certain toxins, not by right atrial distension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The Cushing reflex is triggered by increased intracranial pressure, not by increased atrial pressure. It is a response to brainstem compression and causes hypertension with reflex bradycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The aortic arch baroreceptors detect arterial pressure changes, not right atrial pressure changes. The primary reflex from right atrial distension is the Bainbridge reflex.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with essential hypertension presents for evaluation. Her physician explains that baroreceptors respond to changes in mean arterial pressure by altering their firing rate. At a mean arterial pressure of 60 mm Hg, the baroreceptor firing rate is very low. As MAP increases to 100 mm Hg, the firing rate increases substantially. At a MAP of approximately 200 mm Hg, the firing rate plateaus. Which of the following best describes the effector response when baroreceptor afferent firing reaches its maximum at very high mean arterial pressures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maximal sympathetic activation with increased heart rate, contractility, and vasoconstriction to further elevate blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Equal activation of sympathetic and parasympathetic systems, maintaining blood pressure at its current elevated level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maximal parasympathetic activation of cardiac contractility and vascular smooth muscle to reduce blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complete cessation of all autonomic outflow, leaving the heart and vasculature without neural regulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Minimal sympathetic activity with maximal parasympathetic output, causing bradycardia and vasodilation to reduce blood pressure

explanation.summary: At maximal baroreceptor firing (MAP near 200 mm Hg), the medulla responds with maximal suppression of sympathetic outflow and maximal stimulation of parasympathetic outflow. This produces bradycardia (from parasympathetic effects on SA/AV nodes) and vasodilation (from sympathetic withdrawal), both aimed at reducing blood pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: When baroreceptor afferent firing is maximal due to very high MAP, the medullary response is to maximally suppress sympathetic nervous system activity and maximally stimulate parasympathetic (vagal) activity. Reduced sympathetic output causes vasodilation (decreasing SVR), decreased contractility, and contributes to slowing the heart rate. Increased parasympathetic output via the vagus nerve further slows the heart rate at the SA and AV nodes. Together, these responses produce bradycardia and vasodilation aimed at lowering the elevated blood pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Maximal baroreceptor firing signals high blood pressure, which triggers suppression (not activation) of the sympathetic nervous system. The reflex opposes the elevated pressure, not amplifies it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: At maximal baroreceptor firing, the autonomic response is not balanced. Sympathetic activity is minimized while parasympathetic activity is maximized. Equal activation would not produce the corrective response needed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Complete cessation of all autonomic outflow does not occur. The parasympathetic system is maximally activated while the sympathetic system is minimally active. There is still neural regulation, just shifted toward parasympathetic dominance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While parasympathetic activation does cause bradycardia, the parasympathetic nervous system has minimal direct effect on cardiac contractility and vascular smooth muscle. Vasodilation primarily results from withdrawal of sympathetic vasomotor tone, not from parasympathetic activation of blood vessels.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease (GFR 35 mL/min) presents with worsening bilateral lower extremity edema and blood pressure of 178/102 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show serum sodium 144 mEq/L, serum potassium 5.6 mEq/L, plasma renin activity 0.2 ng/mL/h (low), and serum aldosterone 3 ng/dL (low). Despite the suppressed renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, his blood pressure remains significantly elevated. Which of the following mechanisms is the most likely primary contributor to his hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Persistent activation of angiotensin II AT1 receptors by an alternative non-renin-dependent pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired renal sodium and water excretion leading to volume expansion that is independent of aldosterone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compensatory upregulation of vasopressin V1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle in response to low aldosterone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autonomous adrenal aldosterone secretion that is not reflected in the single serum aldosterone measurement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased sympathetic nervous system activity secondary to hyperkalemia-induced carotid body stimulation

explanation.summary: In advanced chronic kidney disease, the kidneys lose the ability to excrete sodium and water adequately. This leads to volume expansion and hypertension that is independent of the RAAS. The low renin and aldosterone confirm volume-mediated suppression of the axis, but the kidneys cannot respond appropriately to this suppression by excreting the excess volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: With a GFR of 35 mL/min, the kidneys have markedly reduced nephron mass and cannot excrete sodium efficiently. Volume expansion occurs regardless of aldosterone status because the remaining nephrons are unable to handle the filtered sodium load. This volume-dependent hypertension is the hallmark of CKD-associated hypertension and explains why RAAS is suppressed (appropriate response to volume expansion) yet blood pressure remains elevated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While chymase and other non-renin pathways can generate angiotensin II in tissues, the clinical picture with suppressed renin and low aldosterone does not suggest significant angiotensin II activity. If AT1 receptors were being activated, aldosterone would likely be elevated as well.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vasopressin V1 receptor upregulation is not a recognized compensatory mechanism in response to low aldosterone. While vasopressin can cause vasoconstriction, it plays a minor role in chronic blood pressure regulation compared to volume status in CKD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary aldosteronism would present with low renin but elevated aldosterone and hypokalemia. This patient has low aldosterone and hyperkalemia, which is the opposite pattern and consistent with CKD-related suppression of the entire RAAS axis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While sympathetic overactivity does contribute to CKD-associated hypertension, hyperkalemia does not stimulate carotid bodies. Carotid bodies are chemoreceptors sensitive to hypoxia, hypercapnia, and acidosis, not to potassium levels directly.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman is diagnosed with a 2.5-cm aldosterone-producing adenoma of the right adrenal gland. Her blood pressure is 162/98 mm Hg, serum potassium is 2.9 mEq/L, and plasma renin activity is undetectable. She is started on spironolactone prior to planned adrenalectomy. Three weeks later, her potassium normalizes to 4.1 mEq/L, but her blood pressure remains elevated at 152/94 mm Hg. Which of the following best explains the persistent hypertension despite mineralocorticoid receptor blockade?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spironolactone selectively blocks aldosterone effects on potassium secretion but not on sodium reabsorption in the collecting duct

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic aldosterone excess has induced vascular remodeling and endothelial dysfunction that persist despite receptor blockade

choices.label: C
choices.text: The adenoma is co-secreting cortisol, which activates mineralocorticoid receptors that spironolactone cannot block

choices.label: D
choices.text: Persistent suppression of renin has eliminated the normal counter-regulatory vasodilatory effects of angiotensin 1-7

choices.label: E
choices.text: Spironolactone has been converted to an active metabolite that acts as a partial mineralocorticoid receptor agonist

explanation.summary: Chronic aldosterone excess causes structural vascular changes including fibrosis, smooth muscle hypertrophy, and endothelial dysfunction. These changes persist even after mineralocorticoid receptor blockade because the structural remodeling is not rapidly reversible.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prolonged exposure to excess aldosterone causes non-genomic and genomic effects on the vasculature, including vascular smooth muscle hypertrophy, perivascular fibrosis, increased oxidative stress, and reduced nitric oxide bioavailability. These structural and functional changes increase systemic vascular resistance independently of ongoing aldosterone signaling. The potassium correction confirms effective MR blockade in the kidney, but the vascular remodeling requires longer to resolve or may be partially irreversible.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Spironolactone competitively antagonizes the mineralocorticoid receptor in all tissues, affecting both sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion equally. It does not selectively block one effect over the other, as both are mediated through the same nuclear receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While cortisol can activate mineralocorticoid receptors, spironolactone blocks the mineralocorticoid receptor regardless of the ligand (aldosterone or cortisol). Additionally, 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 in the kidney normally inactivates cortisol to cortisone, preventing MR activation by cortisol.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While angiotensin 1-7 has vasodilatory properties, the loss of this counter-regulatory peptide due to low renin is not a major mechanism of persistent hypertension. The primary issue is structural vascular damage from chronic aldosterone excess.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spironolactone's active metabolite, canrenone, is a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, not a partial agonist. All clinically relevant metabolites maintain antagonist activity at the MR.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with congestive heart failure (ejection fraction 25%) and serum sodium of 126 mEq/L is found to have a markedly elevated plasma vasopressin (ADH) level. His serum osmolality is 260 mOsm/kg and urine osmolality is 520 mOsm/kg. Which of the following best explains the physiologic basis for elevated ADH secretion in this patient despite low serum osmolality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced renal perfusion directly stimulates ADH release from the collecting duct via a paracrine mechanism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low cardiac output increases angiotensin II, which directly stimulates ADH release from the posterior pituitary

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased effective arterial blood volume reduces inhibitory input from arterial baroreceptors to the hypothalamus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heart failure activates hepatic osmoreceptors that override the hypothalamic osmoreceptor inhibitory signal

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated atrial natriuretic peptide in heart failure paradoxically stimulates ADH release through natriuretic peptide receptor type C

explanation.summary: In heart failure, reduced cardiac output leads to decreased effective arterial blood volume. This unloads carotid sinus and aortic arch baroreceptors, reducing their tonic inhibitory input to the hypothalamic supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei, thereby stimulating non-osmotic ADH release.
explanation.whyCorrect: Arterial baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch normally send tonic inhibitory signals via cranial nerves IX and X to the hypothalamus, suppressing ADH release. When effective arterial blood volume falls in heart failure, these baroreceptors are unloaded, releasing the hypothalamus from inhibition. This non-osmotic stimulus for ADH release overrides the osmotic inhibition that should occur with hypo-osmolality, resulting in inappropriate water retention and dilutional hyponatremia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ADH (vasopressin) is synthesized in the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary, not from the collecting duct. The collecting duct is the target tissue where ADH acts via V2 receptors, not a site of ADH production.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While angiotensin II can stimulate thirst and has some effect on ADH release, the primary non-osmotic stimulus for ADH in heart failure is the reduced baroreceptor input, not direct angiotensin II stimulation. This is the dominant mechanism and better explains the physiology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepatic osmoreceptors do not exist as a recognized physiologic entity that overrides hypothalamic osmoreceptors. Low-pressure volume receptors exist in the atria and great veins, but the primary mechanism involves arterial baroreceptor unloading.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ANP actually opposes ADH action by promoting natriuresis and diuresis. ANP does not stimulate ADH release; rather, its effects are insufficient to overcome the powerful non-osmotic baroreceptor-mediated ADH release in heart failure.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with episodic headaches, diaphoresis, and palpitations. Her blood pressure is 220/130 mm Hg. Plasma metanephrines are markedly elevated, and imaging reveals a 4-cm right adrenal mass consistent with pheochromocytoma. Prior to surgical removal, she is started on phenoxybenzamine. After two weeks of alpha-blockade, a beta-blocker is added. Which of the following best explains why alpha-adrenergic blockade must precede beta-blockade in this clinical scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-blockers reduce cardiac output, which triggers a reflex increase in catecholamine release from the tumor that overwhelms remaining alpha receptors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-blockers inhibit renin release, causing acute hyperkalemia that potentiates catecholamine-induced coronary vasospasm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction after beta-2 receptor blockade eliminates vasodilatory counterbalance, causing hypertensive crisis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-blockers reduce hepatic clearance of catecholamines by decreasing hepatic blood flow, causing further accumulation of circulating epinephrine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alpha-receptor blockade must be established first to allow adequate volume expansion before reducing heart rate with beta-blockade

explanation.summary: In pheochromocytoma, circulating catecholamines activate both alpha and beta-adrenergic receptors. Beta-2 receptors in skeletal muscle vasculature mediate vasodilation. Blocking beta-2 receptors without prior alpha-blockade removes this vasodilatory counterbalance, leaving alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed, which can precipitate severe hypertensive crisis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epinephrine activates both alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) and beta-2 (vasodilation, primarily in skeletal muscle vasculature) adrenergic receptors. In pheochromocytoma with high circulating epinephrine, beta-2 mediated vasodilation partially offsets alpha-1 vasoconstriction. Administering a non-selective beta-blocker eliminates this vasodilatory effect while alpha-mediated vasoconstriction continues unopposed, leading to a dramatic rise in systemic vascular resistance and potentially fatal hypertensive crisis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pheochromocytoma secretes catecholamines autonomously and is not significantly regulated by reflex mechanisms. The tumor's catecholamine output is not meaningfully increased by reduced cardiac output. The danger lies in the vascular receptor balance, not tumor secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While beta-blockers do reduce renin release, this does not cause acute hyperkalemia of clinical significance in this setting. The risk of beta-blockade before alpha-blockade relates to the vascular alpha/beta receptor balance, not to potassium homeostasis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers reduce hepatic blood flow modestly, but catecholamine metabolism by COMT and MAO is not primarily flow-limited. The risk of beta-blockade first is not related to catecholamine clearance but rather to the loss of beta-2 vasodilatory opposition to alpha vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While volume expansion is indeed important in pheochromocytoma management (patients are often volume-depleted from chronic vasoconstriction), this is not the primary reason alpha-blockade must precede beta-blockade. The immediate danger is the hemodynamic consequence of unopposed alpha-receptor vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with severe preeclampsia at 34 weeks gestation has a blood pressure of 168/110 mm Hg and proteinuria. Laboratory studies show elevated soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase 1 (sFlt-1) and decreased free placental growth factor (PlGF). Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which elevated sFlt-1 contributes to hypertension in preeclampsia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: sFlt-1 directly activates angiotensin II AT1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle, increasing systemic vascular resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: sFlt-1 activates the epithelial sodium channel in the collecting duct, increasing sodium retention independently of aldosterone

choices.label: C
choices.text: sFlt-1 inhibits endothelial nitric oxide synthase by binding to its calmodulin-binding domain

choices.label: D
choices.text: sFlt-1 stimulates aldosterone secretion from the adrenal zona glomerulosa independent of angiotensin II

choices.label: E
choices.text: sFlt-1 sequesters vascular endothelial growth factor, causing endothelial dysfunction and reduced nitric oxide and prostacyclin production

explanation.summary: sFlt-1 is a soluble decoy receptor that binds and sequesters VEGF and PlGF in the circulation, preventing them from activating their endothelial receptors. This causes widespread endothelial dysfunction, reducing production of vasodilators (nitric oxide, prostacyclin) and promoting vasoconstriction and hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: sFlt-1 (soluble VEGF receptor 1) is released in excess from the ischemic placenta in preeclampsia. It acts as a circulating decoy receptor, binding VEGF and PlGF and preventing them from signaling through endothelial VEGF receptors. VEGF normally maintains endothelial health and promotes production of nitric oxide and prostacyclin. Loss of this signaling causes endothelial dysfunction, vasoconstriction, increased vascular permeability, and the clinical features of preeclampsia including hypertension and proteinuria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: sFlt-1 does not directly activate AT1 receptors. It is a soluble VEGF receptor that acts as a decoy, not a ligand for angiotensin receptors. While autoantibodies against AT1 receptors have been described in preeclampsia, this is a separate mechanism from sFlt-1.
whyWrongByChoice.B: sFlt-1 does not directly activate ENaC in the collecting duct. Its pathologic effects are mediated through the vascular endothelium by sequestering angiogenic growth factors, not through direct renal tubular sodium transport effects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: sFlt-1 does not directly inhibit eNOS by binding to calmodulin-binding domains. Rather, it reduces eNOS activity indirectly by preventing VEGF from signaling through its endothelial receptor, which normally upregulates eNOS expression and activity through Akt-mediated phosphorylation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: sFlt-1 does not directly stimulate aldosterone secretion. Its primary mechanism involves sequestration of angiogenic factors from the endothelium, not direct effects on adrenal steroidogenesis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with decompensated heart failure is treated with an intravenous infusion of nesiritide (recombinant B-type natriuretic peptide). Within 30 minutes, his pulmonary capillary wedge pressure decreases from 28 to 18 mm Hg and his systemic vascular resistance falls. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which BNP reduces preload and afterload?

choices.label: A
choices.text: BNP activates guanylyl cyclase-linked natriuretic peptide receptor A, increasing intracellular cGMP in vascular smooth muscle and renal tubular cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: BNP stimulates endothelial nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production and subsequent smooth muscle relaxation

choices.label: C
choices.text: BNP directly inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme activity, reducing angiotensin II formation and aldosterone secretion

choices.label: D
choices.text: BNP activates beta-2 adrenergic receptors on vascular smooth muscle, causing adenylyl cyclase-mediated vasodilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: BNP opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing hyperpolarization and vasodilation

explanation.summary: BNP binds to natriuretic peptide receptor A (NPR-A), which has intrinsic guanylyl cyclase activity. This increases intracellular cGMP, which causes vascular smooth muscle relaxation (reducing preload and afterload) and promotes renal sodium and water excretion (reducing volume).
explanation.whyCorrect: BNP exerts its hemodynamic effects by binding to NPR-A, a transmembrane receptor with a cytoplasmic guanylyl cyclase domain. Receptor activation increases intracellular cGMP, which activates protein kinase G (PKG). In vascular smooth muscle, PKG decreases intracellular calcium, causing relaxation and vasodilation. In the kidney, cGMP increases GFR (afferent arteriolar dilation, efferent constriction) and inhibits sodium reabsorption in the collecting duct. BNP also suppresses renin and aldosterone secretion, further reducing volume.

whyWrongByChoice.B: BNP does not stimulate eNOS. While both BNP and nitric oxide ultimately increase cGMP, they do so through different mechanisms. NO activates soluble guanylyl cyclase, while BNP activates the particulate (membrane-bound) guanylyl cyclase of NPR-A. These are distinct signaling pathways.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BNP does not directly inhibit ACE. While BNP functionally opposes the RAAS by suppressing renin release and aldosterone secretion, these effects are mediated through its own receptor (NPR-A) and cGMP signaling, not through ACE inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BNP does not activate beta-2 adrenergic receptors. Its receptor (NPR-A) is a completely distinct receptor from adrenergic receptors and signals through guanylyl cyclase, not adenylyl cyclase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BNP-mediated vasodilation occurs through cGMP/PKG signaling, not through direct opening of K-ATP channels. K-ATP channels are opened by metabolic signals (decreased ATP/ADP ratio) and by certain drugs like minoxidil, but this is not the mechanism of BNP action.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with resistant hypertension undergoes workup revealing a renal artery stenosis of 80% in the right kidney. His plasma renin activity is elevated. Compared with the contralateral left kidney, which of the following changes is most likely occurring in the stenotic right kidney?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased sodium excretion due to elevated peritubular capillary hydrostatic pressure distal to the stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased renin secretion due to increased sodium delivery to the macula densa from impaired proximal reabsorption

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased sodium reabsorption due to elevated angiotensin II and reduced peritubular capillary hydrostatic pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased glomerular filtration rate with compensatory increase in renal plasma flow to maintain filtration fraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased prostaglandin E2 synthesis causing natriuresis that partially compensates for the angiotensin II-mediated sodium retention

explanation.summary: Distal to a renal artery stenosis, perfusion pressure is reduced. This decreases peritubular capillary hydrostatic pressure while the juxtaglomerular apparatus senses low perfusion and secretes renin. The resulting angiotensin II increases proximal tubular sodium reabsorption, and the low peritubular hydrostatic pressure further favors peritubular capillary reabsorption of sodium and water.
explanation.whyCorrect: The stenosis reduces perfusion pressure to the right kidney, lowering both glomerular filtration and peritubular capillary hydrostatic pressure. The JGA senses low perfusion (reduced stretch in afferent arteriole, reduced NaCl delivery to macula densa) and releases renin. Angiotensin II directly stimulates Na+/H+ exchange in the proximal tubule, enhancing sodium reabsorption. Additionally, the lower peritubular capillary hydrostatic pressure creates a favorable Starling gradient for fluid reabsorption from the interstitium back into the peritubular capillaries. Both mechanisms cause the stenotic kidney to avidly retain sodium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Peritubular capillary hydrostatic pressure is actually decreased distal to the stenosis, not increased. The stenosis causes a pressure drop, which favors sodium reabsorption, not excretion. Increased hydrostatic pressure would promote pressure natriuresis, which is what occurs in the contralateral non-stenotic kidney.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Renin secretion is increased, not decreased, in the stenotic kidney. The reduced perfusion pressure and decreased NaCl delivery to the macula densa (from reduced GFR) both stimulate renin release from the juxtaglomerular cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: GFR is decreased, but renal plasma flow is also decreased distal to the stenosis due to the reduced perfusion pressure. Angiotensin II preferentially constricts the efferent arteriole, which tends to maintain or increase filtration fraction, not decrease it. Renal plasma flow does not compensatorily increase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While prostaglandins are produced in the ischemic kidney and play a protective role in maintaining renal blood flow, their natriuretic effect is insufficient to overcome the powerful sodium-retaining effects of angiotensin II and aldosterone in this setting.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying the effects of a novel drug that selectively inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) in animal models. Compared with untreated controls, which of the following cardiovascular changes would be most expected in the treated animals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased blood pressure due to reduced conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased blood pressure due to accumulation of bradykinin from reduced degradation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased blood pressure due to enhanced aldosterone release from direct ACE2-mediated adrenal stimulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased blood pressure due to accumulation of angiotensin II and reduced formation of angiotensin 1-7

choices.label: E
choices.text: No significant change in blood pressure because ACE2 has no role in the renin-angiotensin system

explanation.summary: ACE2 converts angiotensin II to angiotensin 1-7, which is a vasodilatory, anti-inflammatory, and anti-proliferative peptide. Inhibiting ACE2 would cause accumulation of angiotensin II (a potent vasoconstrictor) and reduce levels of the protective angiotensin 1-7, leading to increased blood pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: ACE2 is a carboxypeptidase distinct from ACE. Its primary function in the RAAS is to cleave angiotensin II into angiotensin 1-7. Angiotensin 1-7 acts on Mas receptors to cause vasodilation, anti-inflammation, and anti-fibrosis. By inhibiting ACE2, angiotensin II accumulates (promoting vasoconstriction, sodium retention, and fibrosis) while the counter-regulatory angiotensin 1-7 pathway is diminished. This shifts the RAAS balance toward the vasoconstrictive/pro-hypertensive axis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACE2 does not convert angiotensin I to angiotensin II; that is the function of ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme). ACE2 is a different enzyme that primarily degrades angiotensin II to angiotensin 1-7. Inhibiting ACE2 would not reduce angiotensin II formation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ACE2 does not degrade bradykinin. Bradykinin is degraded by ACE (kininase II). Therefore, ACE2 inhibition would not affect bradykinin levels. Accumulation of bradykinin is a feature of ACE inhibitor therapy, not ACE2 inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ACE2 does not directly stimulate the adrenal gland to release aldosterone. Aldosterone secretion is primarily regulated by angiotensin II, potassium levels, and ACTH. While ACE2 inhibition would increase angiotensin II (which stimulates aldosterone), the mechanism described is incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ACE2 plays an important counter-regulatory role in the RAAS by converting angiotensin II to the vasodilatory peptide angiotensin 1-7. Studies in ACE2 knockout mice demonstrate elevated blood pressure, confirming its physiologic significance in blood pressure regulation.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with essential hypertension is started on lisinopril 10 mg daily. After two weeks, his blood pressure has improved from 158/96 to 138/88 mm Hg. Laboratory values show serum potassium increased from 4.0 to 4.8 mEq/L and serum creatinine increased from 1.0 to 1.3 mg/dL. Which of the following best explains the rise in serum creatinine observed with ACE inhibitor therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct nephrotoxic effect of the ACE inhibitor on proximal tubular epithelial cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduced aldosterone-mediated sodium reabsorption causing volume depletion and prerenal azotemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilation of the efferent arteriole reducing intraglomerular pressure and glomerular filtration rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constriction of the afferent arteriole due to loss of prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased mesangial cell contraction reducing the glomerular capillary surface area available for filtration

explanation.summary: ACE inhibitors reduce angiotensin II production. Because angiotensin II preferentially constricts the efferent arteriole, ACE inhibition causes efferent arteriolar dilation. This reduces intraglomerular hydrostatic pressure and GFR, reflected by a modest rise in serum creatinine. This effect is generally hemodynamic and reversible.
explanation.whyCorrect: Angiotensin II constricts both afferent and efferent glomerular arterioles, but its effect on the efferent arteriole is disproportionately greater. This efferent constriction maintains intraglomerular pressure and GFR. When ACE inhibitors reduce angiotensin II, the efferent arteriole dilates, lowering the glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure and reducing the net filtration pressure. The resulting decrease in GFR is reflected by a rise in creatinine, which is typically modest (up to 30%) and is considered an expected hemodynamic effect rather than renal injury.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACE inhibitors are not directly nephrotoxic. They do not cause tubular epithelial cell injury. In fact, ACE inhibitors are renoprotective in chronic kidney disease by reducing intraglomerular hypertension and proteinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone and can cause mild volume reduction, the primary mechanism of the creatinine rise is the hemodynamic effect on the efferent arteriole. A 30% creatinine rise from pure volume depletion would require more significant dehydration than is typically caused by aldosterone reduction alone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ACE inhibitors do not constrict the afferent arteriole. If anything, by increasing bradykinin (which ACE normally degrades), ACE inhibitors may promote some afferent arteriolar vasodilation. The key mechanism is efferent dilation, not afferent constriction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Angiotensin II causes mesangial cell contraction, which reduces filtration surface area. ACE inhibition would relax mesangial cells, increasing the filtration coefficient. This effect would tend to increase, not decrease, GFR and thus does not explain the rise in creatinine.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of bilateral renal artery stenosis is inadvertently started on enalapril for blood pressure control. Within 48 hours, her serum creatinine rises from 1.4 to 4.8 mg/dL and urine output falls to 200 mL/day. Which of the following best explains why ACE inhibitor-induced acute kidney injury is particularly severe in bilateral renal artery stenosis compared with unilateral disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both kidneys depend on angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriolar tone to maintain GFR across the stenotic lesions, and no contralateral kidney can compensate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bilateral stenosis causes higher circulating renin levels that produce toxic concentrations of angiotensin 1-7 when ACE is inhibited

choices.label: C
choices.text: ACE inhibitors cause bilateral afferent arteriolar constriction that is additive with the fixed stenotic lesions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bilateral renal artery stenosis produces higher bradykinin levels after ACE inhibition, causing anaphylactoid-mediated glomerular damage

choices.label: E
choices.text: The reduction in aldosterone affects both kidneys' ability to concentrate urine, causing obligatory water loss and prerenal azotemia

explanation.summary: In bilateral renal artery stenosis, both kidneys have reduced perfusion pressure and depend on angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction to maintain intraglomerular pressure and GFR. ACE inhibition removes this compensatory mechanism from both kidneys simultaneously, and there is no unaffected contralateral kidney to maintain overall renal function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Behind a renal artery stenosis, perfusion pressure is critically reduced. GFR is maintained by angiotensin II-mediated constriction of the efferent arteriole, which sustains intraglomerular hydrostatic pressure despite low perfusion. In unilateral stenosis, ACE inhibition may impair GFR in the stenotic kidney, but the contralateral normal kidney compensates by increasing its filtration. In bilateral stenosis, ACE inhibition causes GFR to fall precipitously in both kidneys simultaneously, with no compensatory reserve, leading to oliguric acute kidney injury.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Angiotensin 1-7 is a vasodilatory, protective peptide and is not nephrotoxic at any concentration. Furthermore, ACE inhibition reduces angiotensin II but increases angiotensin I, which is then preferentially converted to angiotensin 1-9 by ACE2, not to toxic levels of angiotensin 1-7.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ACE inhibitors do not cause afferent arteriolar constriction. They reduce angiotensin II, which preferentially dilates the efferent arteriole. The afferent arteriole tone is primarily regulated by myogenic mechanisms and tubuloglomerular feedback.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While bradykinin levels do increase with ACE inhibition, bradykinin does not cause anaphylactoid glomerular damage. Bradykinin-related effects of ACE inhibitors include cough and rare angioedema, but not direct glomerular injury.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The acute kidney injury in this scenario is caused by a dramatic fall in GFR from loss of efferent tone, not by impaired urine concentration. Aldosterone-related effects on concentration would cause mild polyuria, not oliguric renal failure with creatinine rising to 4.8 mg/dL.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male athlete begins a maximal sprint exercise. Within seconds, blood flow to the active skeletal muscle increases approximately 20-fold. During the initial seconds of exercise, before systemic hemodynamic adjustments occur, which of the following is the predominant mechanism responsible for the rapid increase in local skeletal muscle blood flow?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Release of nitric oxide from endothelial cells in response to increased shear stress from rising cardiac output

choices.label: B
choices.text: Withdrawal of sympathetic vasoconstrictor tone to skeletal muscle arterioles via a central command mechanism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Local accumulation of metabolites including potassium, adenosine, and hydrogen ions causing arteriolar smooth muscle relaxation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Activation of beta-2 adrenergic receptors on skeletal muscle arterioles by circulating epinephrine from the adrenal medulla

choices.label: E
choices.text: Histamine release from local mast cells activated by mechanical compression during muscle contraction

explanation.summary: The rapid increase in skeletal muscle blood flow at the onset of exercise is primarily driven by metabolic vasodilation. Contracting muscle rapidly releases potassium, adenosine, CO2, hydrogen ions, and phosphate, which act directly on arteriolar smooth muscle to cause relaxation and vasodilation, increasing local blood flow to match metabolic demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: During the initial seconds of intense exercise, active skeletal muscle metabolism outstrips oxygen delivery, leading to rapid local accumulation of vasodilatory metabolites. Potassium is released from depolarizing muscle fibers, adenosine accumulates from ATP hydrolysis, and hydrogen ions/CO2 increase from accelerated metabolism. These metabolites act directly on arteriolar smooth muscle, causing relaxation by opening potassium channels and inhibiting calcium entry. This metabolic vasodilation is the fastest local mechanism and does not require neural or systemic hormonal input.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Shear stress-mediated NO release does contribute to exercise hyperemia but is a secondary response that amplifies and sustains the initial metabolic vasodilation. In the initial seconds, before cardiac output has increased significantly, shear stress has not yet risen substantially in muscle arterioles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sympathetic tone to skeletal muscle actually increases during exercise as part of the generalized sympathetic activation. The vasodilation occurs despite this increased sympathetic tone because local metabolites override sympathetic vasoconstriction through functional sympatholysis. Withdrawal of sympathetic tone is not the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Circulating epinephrine from the adrenal medulla takes longer to rise and is part of the systemic hemodynamic response, not the immediate local response. Furthermore, during maximal exercise, sympathetic alpha-1 vasoconstriction in non-exercising beds predominates over beta-2 effects. The immediate muscle vasodilation is metabolic, not adrenergic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mast cell degranulation and histamine release are not a recognized mechanism of exercise-induced vasodilation. Mast cells release histamine in allergic/inflammatory responses, not in response to mechanical muscle contraction during normal exercise.

questions.stem: An investigator isolates a small arteriole from the cerebral vasculature and increases the intraluminal pressure from 60 to 100 mm Hg. The vessel responds with a sustained constriction that reduces its diameter by 15%. Which of the following best describes the cellular mechanism underlying this response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased transmural pressure activates stretch-sensitive cation channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing depolarization and calcium influx through L-type voltage-gated calcium channels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated pressure increases endothelial shear stress, stimulating endothelin-1 release that binds ETA receptors on smooth muscle causing sustained contraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: The pressure increase compresses adventitial nerve endings, triggering local norepinephrine release and alpha-1 receptor-mediated vasoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Higher intraluminal pressure increases oxygen delivery beyond metabolic demand, causing local hyperoxia-induced generation of reactive oxygen species that inactivate nitric oxide

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pressure-induced stretch of the internal elastic lamina releases stored calcium from the elastic fibers, which diffuses to adjacent smooth muscle cells

explanation.summary: The myogenic response (Bayliss effect) is an intrinsic property of vascular smooth muscle. Increased transmural pressure stretches smooth muscle cells, activating mechanosensitive (stretch-activated) cation channels. The resulting depolarization opens voltage-gated L-type calcium channels, increasing intracellular calcium and causing sustained vasoconstriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The myogenic response is a fundamental mechanism of autoregulation, particularly important in the cerebral and renal circulations. When intraluminal pressure increases, vascular smooth muscle cells are stretched. This activates stretch-sensitive nonselective cation channels (likely TRPC6 and TRPM4), which depolarize the cell membrane. Depolarization activates L-type voltage-gated calcium channels (CaV1.2), causing calcium influx and smooth muscle contraction. This response is entirely intrinsic to smooth muscle and occurs in isolated vessels without endothelium or innervation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While endothelin-1 is a potent vasoconstrictor, the myogenic response occurs in denuded vessels without endothelium, demonstrating it is independent of endothelial factors. Additionally, increased intraluminal pressure does not necessarily increase shear stress; shear stress depends on flow velocity, not pressure directly.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The myogenic response is not neurally mediated. It occurs in isolated vessels in vitro, completely removed from neural influences. The response is intrinsic to vascular smooth muscle cells and does not require adventitial nerve endings or norepinephrine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While metabolic mechanisms do contribute to autoregulation, the myogenic response is a pressure-sensing mechanism, not a metabolite-sensing mechanism. The vessel constricts in response to pressure itself, independent of oxygen levels or metabolic byproducts. This is demonstrated in isolated vessels perfused with physiologic buffer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The internal elastic lamina does not store calcium or release it in response to stretch. Intracellular calcium in smooth muscle is stored in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, and extracellular calcium enters through membrane channels. Elastic fibers provide structural support but are not calcium storage sites.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with stable angina undergoes coronary angiography showing a 70% stenosis of the left anterior descending artery. Despite the stenosis, resting myocardial perfusion imaging is normal. When adenosine is administered intravenously as part of a pharmacologic stress test, a perfusion defect appears in the LAD territory. Which of the following best explains why the perfusion defect becomes apparent only during adenosine-mediated vasodilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adenosine causes coronary vasoconstriction in territories supplied by stenotic arteries due to endothelial dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adenosine directly increases myocardial oxygen consumption, creating supply-demand mismatch only in the stenotic territory

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adenosine activates sympathetic reflexes that preferentially constrict collateral vessels supplying the ischemic territory

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adenosine maximally dilates arterioles in non-stenotic territories, increasing flow disparity because the post-stenotic bed is already maximally dilated at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adenosine inhibits autoregulation in all territories, but the stenotic territory loses flow because the fixed lesion prevents compensatory upstream dilation

explanation.summary: This describes the concept of coronary steal. At rest, arterioles in the post-stenotic territory are already maximally dilated (autoregulatory reserve exhausted) to maintain adequate perfusion despite the proximal stenosis. When adenosine is administered, arterioles in normal territories dilate further, dramatically increasing their flow. The post-stenotic territory cannot increase flow further, creating a flow disparity that appears as a relative perfusion defect.
explanation.whyCorrect: Behind a hemodynamically significant stenosis, the coronary microcirculation is already dilated at rest to compensate for the reduced perfusion pressure (autoregulatory reserve is partially or fully exhausted). Adenosine, a potent arteriolar vasodilator, maximally dilates arterioles in normally perfused territories, increasing their blood flow 3-5 fold. The post-stenotic bed, already near-maximally dilated, cannot increase flow proportionally. This creates a flow heterogeneity (coronary steal phenomenon) that is detected as a relative perfusion defect on imaging.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenosine does not cause vasoconstriction in post-stenotic territories. It is a vasodilator in all coronary territories. The perfusion defect is a relative phenomenon due to differential vasodilatory reserve, not due to paradoxical vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Adenosine does not significantly increase myocardial oxygen demand. Unlike dobutamine stress testing, which increases heart rate and contractility, adenosine causes vasodilation without substantially increasing cardiac work. This is precisely why adenosine stress tests are useful in patients who cannot exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adenosine does not cause sympathetic-mediated collateral vessel constriction. While adenosine can cause mild hypotension with reflex sympathetic activation, this does not selectively constrict collateral vessels to ischemic territories.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mechanism is not about upstream dilation being prevented by the stenosis. The fixed stenosis limits maximal flow through the entire vascular bed. The key concept is that the post-stenotic microcirculation has already used its vasodilatory reserve at rest, so additional systemic vasodilation preferentially increases flow to normal territories.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman donates blood (500 mL). Despite the reduction in circulating volume, blood flow to her brain remains essentially unchanged over the next several minutes. Which of the following pairs of mechanisms is most responsible for maintaining constant cerebral blood flow in the face of reduced mean arterial pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased cerebral venous constriction to reduce outflow and baroreceptor-mediated increase in cardiac contractility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myogenic vasodilation of cerebral arterioles in response to reduced transmural pressure and metabolic vasodilation from local CO2 accumulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sympathetic vasoconstriction of cerebral arterioles to maintain vessel wall tension and increased erythropoietin-mediated oxygen carrying capacity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opening of cerebral arteriovenous anastomoses to bypass the capillary bed and endothelin-mediated precapillary sphincter relaxation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Release of atrial natriuretic peptide causing selective cerebral vasodilation and angiotensin II-mediated redirection of cardiac output to the brain

explanation.summary: Cerebral autoregulation maintains constant blood flow across a range of mean arterial pressures (approximately 60-150 mm Hg) through two primary intrinsic mechanisms: the myogenic response (arterioles dilate when transmural pressure decreases) and metabolic regulation (local CO2 and other metabolite accumulation causes vasodilation when flow is reduced).
explanation.whyCorrect: When blood pressure decreases after blood donation, cerebral perfusion pressure falls. Two complementary mechanisms maintain flow: (1) The myogenic response causes cerebral arteriolar smooth muscle to relax when transmural pressure decreases, reducing vascular resistance. (2) Even a brief reduction in flow allows local CO2 and other metabolites (H+, adenosine, K+) to accumulate, which are potent cerebral vasodilators. Together, these mechanisms decrease cerebrovascular resistance proportionally to the pressure reduction, maintaining constant flow within the autoregulatory range.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cerebral veins do not actively constrict to maintain cerebral blood flow. Autoregulation is accomplished at the arteriolar level through changes in resistance. Venous constriction would actually impede cerebral outflow and increase intracranial pressure, which is counterproductive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cerebral arterioles have relatively sparse sympathetic innervation, and sympathetic vasoconstriction plays a minor role in cerebral autoregulation compared to myogenic and metabolic mechanisms. Furthermore, sympathetic activation would vasoconstrict, reducing flow, which is the opposite of the needed response. Erythropoietin acts over days to weeks, not minutes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The cerebral circulation lacks significant arteriovenous anastomoses. These structures are found in the skin (for thermoregulation) but not in the brain. Additionally, precapillary sphincters as discrete structures are not a prominent feature of the cerebral microcirculation, and endothelin is a vasoconstrictor, not a relaxant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ANP does not cause selective cerebral vasodilation. ANP levels would actually decrease, not increase, after blood loss due to reduced atrial stretch. Furthermore, angiotensin II does not selectively redirect cardiac output to the brain; cerebral autoregulation is locally mediated, not hormonally directed.

questions.stem: A researcher perfuses an isolated segment of mesenteric arteriole with a solution and progressively increases the flow rate while keeping intraluminal pressure constant. As flow rate increases, the vessel diameter progressively increases. This flow-mediated dilation is abolished when the endothelium is mechanically removed. Which of the following best describes the signaling cascade responsible for this flow-dependent vasodilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased flow velocity activates endothelial piezo1 channels, causing calcium influx that stimulates phospholipase A2 and cyclooxygenase-mediated prostacyclin synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shear stress on the endothelial glycocalyx is transduced to the cytoskeleton, activating endothelial nitric oxide synthase through phosphorylation by Akt kinase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Laminar flow causes endothelial cell alignment that opens gap junctions between endothelial and smooth muscle cells, directly hyperpolarizing the smooth muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flow-induced turbulence generates reactive oxygen species in endothelial cells that activate soluble guanylyl cyclase in adjacent smooth muscle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased flow velocity reduces endothelial endothelin-1 secretion, removing tonic vasoconstrictor input to underlying smooth muscle

explanation.summary: Flow-mediated dilation is primarily mediated by endothelial shear stress sensing. The glycocalyx and mechanosensory complex on endothelial cells detect shear stress and activate intracellular signaling through PI3K/Akt, which phosphorylates eNOS at serine 1177, increasing nitric oxide production. NO diffuses to smooth muscle, activates soluble guanylyl cyclase, increases cGMP, and causes relaxation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The endothelial glycocalyx acts as a primary mechanosensor, transmitting shear stress to the cytoskeleton and intracellular signaling complexes (including PECAM-1, VE-cadherin, and VEGFR2). This activates the PI3K/Akt pathway, which phosphorylates eNOS at serine 1177, increasing its catalytic activity. The resulting NO diffuses to vascular smooth muscle and activates soluble guanylyl cyclase, increasing cGMP and activating PKG, which reduces intracellular calcium and causes smooth muscle relaxation. This is the dominant mechanism of flow-mediated dilation and is abolished by endothelial removal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While Piezo1 channels are mechanosensitive and present in endothelial cells, and prostacyclin does contribute to flow-mediated responses, the dominant mechanism of flow-mediated dilation is the eNOS/NO/cGMP pathway, not the prostacyclin pathway. Studies with NOS inhibitors (L-NAME) abolish most of the flow-mediated dilation in conduit arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While myoendothelial gap junctions do exist and can transmit hyperpolarizing signals (EDHF pathway), flow-mediated dilation is primarily NO-dependent, not gap junction-dependent. Cell alignment in response to laminar flow is a chronic adaptive response, not the acute mechanism of flow-mediated dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Laminar flow (not turbulent flow) is the stimulus for flow-mediated dilation. Turbulent flow actually promotes endothelial dysfunction and oxidative stress, which would impair vasodilation rather than promote it. Soluble guanylyl cyclase is activated by NO, not by reactive oxygen species.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While shear stress can modulate endothelin-1 expression, the primary acute mechanism of flow-mediated dilation is active NO production, not passive withdrawal of endothelin-1. The immediate vasodilation upon increased flow is too rapid to be explained by reduced endothelin transcription and secretion.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes undergoes assessment of forearm vascular function. Reactive hyperemia testing shows a markedly blunted flow response after 5 minutes of brachial artery occlusion compared with age-matched non-diabetic controls. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms best explains the impaired reactive hyperemia in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic hyperglycemia-induced formation of advanced glycation end products causing reduced endothelial nitric oxide bioavailability and impaired smooth muscle relaxation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diabetic autonomic neuropathy causing excessive parasympathetic vasodilatory tone at baseline, leaving no reserve for reactive hyperemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insulin-mediated activation of endothelin receptors on arteriolar smooth muscle causing fixed vasoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperglycemia-induced reduction in circulating red blood cell mass causing chronic anemia and compensatory vasodilation at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diabetes-related overexpression of prostaglandin H2 synthase increasing prostacyclin production and causing tachyphylaxis to vasodilatory stimuli

explanation.summary: Chronic hyperglycemia leads to formation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and increased oxidative stress. AGEs bind to RAGE receptors on endothelial cells, increasing superoxide production, which rapidly inactivates nitric oxide. The resulting endothelial dysfunction impairs the NO-dependent vasodilation that is a major component of reactive hyperemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reactive hyperemia depends heavily on endothelial NO production, metabolic vasodilators, and myogenic responses. In diabetes, AGEs accumulate in vessel walls and activate RAGE receptors, generating superoxide via NADPH oxidase. Superoxide reacts with NO to form peroxynitrite, reducing NO bioavailability. Additionally, AGEs cause eNOS uncoupling (converting it from an NO producer to a superoxide producer), further compounding endothelial dysfunction. Hyperglycemia also impairs smooth muscle responsiveness through PKC activation and altered ion channel function. These combined effects markedly blunt the vasodilatory capacity during reactive hyperemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Diabetic autonomic neuropathy typically reduces both sympathetic and parasympathetic function. The parasympathetic nervous system plays a minimal role in peripheral vascular tone (it primarily affects the heart). Peripheral vasodilation is mediated by local metabolic factors and endothelial function, not parasympathetic innervation of resistance vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Insulin actually promotes vasodilation through endothelial NO production via the PI3K/Akt/eNOS pathway. In insulin resistance, this vasodilatory effect is impaired. Insulin does not activate endothelin receptors to cause fixed vasoconstriction; rather, in diabetes, the balance shifts from insulin's vasodilatory pathway toward its MAPK/endothelin-1 pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diabetes does not characteristically cause reduced red blood cell mass or chronic anemia through hyperglycemia alone. While some diabetic patients may have anemia from CKD, this is not the primary mechanism of impaired reactive hyperemia, which is fundamentally a vascular endothelial dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prostaglandin H2 synthase (COX) produces PGH2, which can be converted to either prostacyclin (vasodilatory) or thromboxane A2 (vasoconstrictive). In diabetes, the balance shifts toward thromboxane production, not increased prostacyclin. Even if prostacyclin were increased, tachyphylaxis to vasodilatory prostanoids is not a recognized mechanism of diabetic vascular dysfunction.

questions.stem: During a laboratory exercise, a medical student observes a frog mesenteric microcirculation preparation under a microscope. After topical application of histamine, she observes increased spacing between endothelial cells in postcapillary venules, with visible extravasation of fluorescent albumin into the interstitial space. The arterioles and capillaries show no change in permeability. Which of the following best explains why postcapillary venules are the primary site of increased vascular permeability in response to inflammatory mediators?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Postcapillary venules have the highest density of histamine H1 receptors, which activate endothelial cell contraction through myosin light chain phosphorylation

choices.label: B
choices.text: The low hydrostatic pressure and low shear stress in postcapillary venules allow inflammatory mediator-induced gap formation to persist without being sealed by flow forces

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postcapillary venules lack a continuous basement membrane, making them inherently more permeable than upstream segments even without inflammatory stimulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Venular endothelial cells express more aquaporin-1 channels, which histamine converts from water-only channels to non-selective pores permeable to albumin

choices.label: E
choices.text: The postcapillary venule has the thinnest smooth muscle layer, allowing histamine to bypass vascular smooth muscle and directly contact endothelial cells

explanation.summary: Postcapillary venules are the primary site of inflammatory-mediated permeability changes because their endothelial cells have the highest concentration of histamine H1 receptors. Activation of these receptors triggers intracellular calcium elevation, myosin light chain kinase activation, and endothelial cell contraction, pulling cells apart and forming intercellular gaps that allow plasma protein extravasation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Histamine binds to H1 receptors on venular endothelial cells, activating phospholipase C and increasing intracellular calcium via IP3-mediated release from the endoplasmic reticulum. Elevated calcium activates myosin light chain kinase (MLCK), which phosphorylates myosin light chains in endothelial cells. This causes actin-myosin contraction of the endothelial cell cytoskeleton, pulling cells apart at their junctions and creating inter-endothelial gaps. The highest density of H1 receptors is found on postcapillary venular endothelium, explaining the site-specific permeability response.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While hemodynamic conditions in venules differ from arterioles, low shear stress alone does not explain the site-specificity of the permeability response. The primary determinant is receptor density and endothelial cell contractile response, not passive hemodynamic factors. Gaps form actively through receptor-mediated signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Postcapillary venules do have a basement membrane, though it may be thinner than in some other vascular segments. The inflammatory permeability response is not due to absent basement membrane but rather to active endothelial cell contraction creating intercellular gaps through which protein-rich fluid passes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aquaporin-1 channels are water-selective channels and do not convert to non-selective pores in response to histamine. Inflammatory permeability increases occur through intercellular gaps between contracted endothelial cells, not through transcellular aquaporin channels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Histamine acts on endothelial cells directly from the luminal (blood) side or from the abluminal side after release from perivascular mast cells. It does not need to bypass smooth muscle because it acts on endothelial receptors. The thin smooth muscle layer is not relevant to histamine's access to endothelial H1 receptors.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man undergoes a treadmill exercise test. His resting heart rate is 72 beats/min and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. At peak exercise, his heart rate reaches 165 beats/min and blood pressure is 190/78 mm Hg. Despite the marked increase in systemic blood pressure, blood flow to the exercising skeletal muscles continues to increase. Which of the following best explains the ability of exercising muscle to override the expected myogenic vasoconstriction that elevated arterial pressure would normally trigger?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exercise-induced release of cortisol blocks the L-type calcium channels required for the myogenic response in skeletal muscle arterioles

choices.label: B
choices.text: The increased cardiac output during exercise reduces mean circulatory filling pressure, negating the pressure signal for the myogenic response

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skeletal muscle arterioles lack the stretch-activated channels necessary for the myogenic response, which is unique to cerebral and renal vessels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased venous return during exercise raises venous pressure in muscle, equalizing transmural pressure and eliminating the stimulus for myogenic constriction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Local metabolite accumulation produces functional sympatholysis and overcomes the myogenic vasoconstrictor response through metabolic vasodilation

explanation.summary: During exercise, the massive accumulation of local metabolites (K+, adenosine, H+, CO2, inorganic phosphate) in active skeletal muscle overwhelms both the myogenic vasoconstrictor response and sympathetic vasoconstrictor tone. This metabolic vasodilation dominates local vascular control, a phenomenon known as functional sympatholysis, ensuring blood flow matches the tissue's metabolic demands.
explanation.whyCorrect: During vigorous exercise, contracting skeletal muscle produces large quantities of vasodilatory metabolites that act directly on arteriolar smooth muscle. These metabolites (particularly K+, adenosine, H+, and inorganic phosphate) hyperpolarize smooth muscle cells by opening K+ channels, reducing calcium entry and overriding the depolarization and calcium influx that would otherwise occur from the myogenic response. This metabolic override ensures that blood flow increases in proportion to metabolic demand, even when systemic pressure rises. The term functional sympatholysis describes how these metabolites also overcome sympathetic vasoconstriction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cortisol does not block L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle. While cortisol is released during exercise as part of the stress response, its vascular effects are primarily related to permissive actions on catecholamine sensitivity and are not the mechanism by which exercising muscle overrides the myogenic response.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mean circulatory filling pressure actually tends to increase during exercise due to sympathetic venoconstriction, which mobilizes venous blood to maintain cardiac output. Furthermore, the myogenic response is determined by local transmural pressure at the arteriolar level, not by mean circulatory filling pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Skeletal muscle arterioles do possess stretch-activated channels and can exhibit myogenic responses. The myogenic response is present throughout the systemic vasculature, not limited to cerebral and renal vessels. In exercising muscle, this response is simply overwhelmed by the dominant metabolic vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While muscle contraction does intermittently compress veins, the net venous pressure in exercising muscle does not rise sufficiently to equalize transmural pressure across arterioles. The transmural pressure across arterioles is determined primarily by intraluminal arterial pressure minus tissue pressure, and this gradient still favors myogenic constriction. The override is metabolic, not mechanical.

questions.stem: A newborn infant is diagnosed with persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN). Despite delivery and establishment of breathing with 100% oxygen, the pulmonary vascular resistance remains elevated. Inhaled nitric oxide is administered. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which inhaled NO selectively reduces pulmonary vascular resistance without causing systemic hypotension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhaled NO is rapidly converted to nitrate by pulmonary epithelial cells and excreted renally before reaching the systemic circulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: NO preferentially activates particulate guanylyl cyclase in pulmonary arterioles, which is not present in systemic arterioles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhaled NO reaches only ventilated lung regions where it diffuses into adjacent pulmonary vascular smooth muscle, and is rapidly inactivated by binding to hemoglobin upon entering the bloodstream

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhaled NO is selectively absorbed by pulmonary surfactant, which delivers it to type II pneumocytes that release a secondary vasodilatory mediator locally

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pulmonary circulation expresses higher levels of phosphodiesterase-3, making it more responsive to cGMP-mediated vasodilation than systemic vessels

explanation.summary: Inhaled NO is delivered directly to the alveoli, where it diffuses across the alveolar membrane into adjacent pulmonary arteriolar smooth muscle, causing local vasodilation via soluble guanylyl cyclase/cGMP. When NO enters the bloodstream, it is immediately bound and inactivated by hemoglobin (forming methemoglobin and nitrate), preventing systemic vasodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inhaled NO is a gas that reaches ventilated alveoli and diffuses the short distance into the smooth muscle of adjacent pulmonary arterioles. There it activates soluble guanylyl cyclase, increasing cGMP and causing vasodilation. This also has the beneficial effect of redirecting blood flow to ventilated areas (improving V/Q matching). Once NO diffuses into the pulmonary capillary blood, it has an extremely high affinity for hemoglobin (reaction rate ~10^7 M^-1 s^-1), binding to the heme iron and forming nitrosyl-hemoglobin or being oxidized to methemoglobin and nitrate. This rapid inactivation prevents any NO from reaching the systemic circulation in active form.

whyWrongByChoice.A: NO is not converted to nitrate by pulmonary epithelial cells. It is inactivated by binding to hemoglobin in the blood, not by epithelial metabolism. The hemoglobin-mediated inactivation is nearly instantaneous and is the key mechanism ensuring pulmonary selectivity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: NO activates soluble guanylyl cyclase (sGC), not particulate guanylyl cyclase. Particulate guanylyl cyclase is the receptor for natriuretic peptides (ANP, BNP). sGC is present in both pulmonary and systemic vascular smooth muscle. The selectivity is due to the route of delivery and hemoglobin inactivation, not receptor distribution.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surfactant does not selectively absorb NO or deliver it to type II pneumocytes. NO is a small gaseous molecule that freely diffuses across alveolar membranes. Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant but do not serve as intermediaries for NO-mediated vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE5), not PDE3, is the primary enzyme that degrades cGMP in pulmonary vascular smooth muscle. While PDE5 expression is high in pulmonary vessels, the selectivity of inhaled NO is due to its route of delivery and rapid hemoglobin inactivation, not differential PDE expression.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man sustains a deep laceration to his forearm. Initially, the wound bleeds briskly. Within minutes, he notices that the bleeding has slowed considerably, and the skin surrounding the wound appears pale and blanched. Which of the following best describes the local vascular mechanism most responsible for the immediate reduction in blood flow to the injured area?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thromboxane A2 released from activated platelets at the wound site causes potent vasoconstriction of upstream arterioles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Local release of serotonin from dense granules of activated platelets stimulates 5-HT2 receptors on vascular smooth muscle causing vasoconstriction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endothelin-1 released from damaged endothelial cells binds to ETA receptors on vascular smooth muscle, producing sustained vasoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct myogenic constriction of arteriolar smooth muscle in response to the acute drop in transmural pressure caused by hemorrhage from the wound

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sympathetic axon reflex through perivascular sensory nerve fibers causing local norepinephrine release and alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction

explanation.summary: When blood vessels are damaged, injured endothelial cells release endothelin-1, the most potent known vasoconstrictor peptide. Endothelin-1 binds to ETA receptors on vascular smooth muscle, causing intense and sustained vasoconstriction of the injured vessel and surrounding arterioles. This is a critical component of the immediate hemostatic vascular response to injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endothelin-1 (ET-1) is constitutively produced by endothelial cells and stored in Weibel-Palade bodies. Vascular injury causes release of ET-1 from damaged endothelium. ET-1 binds to ETA receptors on vascular smooth muscle, activating phospholipase C, increasing IP3 and intracellular calcium, causing powerful, sustained vasoconstriction. This is considered the primary endothelium-derived vasoconstrictor responsible for the vascular spasm component of hemostasis. The sustained nature of ET-1-mediated constriction (lasting minutes to hours) matches the clinical observation of prolonged local blanching around a wound.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While TxA2 from platelets does contribute to vasoconstriction during hemostasis, platelet activation and aggregation require initial contact with exposed subendothelial collagen and take slightly longer to develop than the immediate endothelin-1 release from damaged endothelium. TxA2 is more important for amplifying platelet aggregation than for the initial vascular spasm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Serotonin from platelet dense granules does cause vasoconstriction and contributes to hemostasis, but like TxA2, this requires prior platelet activation at the injury site. The immediate vascular response is driven by factors released directly from the damaged vessel wall, particularly endothelin-1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The myogenic response causes vasoconstriction in response to increased transmural pressure, not decreased pressure. A drop in transmural pressure (from hemorrhage) would actually cause myogenic vasodilation, which is the opposite of the observed response. The vasoconstriction here is chemically mediated, not myogenically mediated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An axon reflex involving sensory nerves typically releases substance P and CGRP, which are vasodilatory neuropeptides causing the flare response. Sympathetic vasoconstriction via norepinephrine is a systemic reflex that acts through the spinal cord, not a local axon reflex. The immediate local vasoconstriction at a wound is primarily mediated by endothelial and platelet-derived factors.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with a 15-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with bilateral lower extremity swelling that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. Her serum albumin is 2.1 g/dL (normal: 3.5–5.5 g/dL), and a 24-hour urine collection shows 5.2 g of protein. Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane with subepithelial deposits. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this patient's edema?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased hydrostatic pressure in the glomerular capillaries causing filtration of excess fluid into the interstitial space of the lower extremities

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased plasma oncotic pressure from urinary protein losses reducing the osmotic force that retains fluid within the capillary lumen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased capillary permeability from diabetic microangiopathy allowing albumin to leak into the interstitial space across peripheral capillaries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obstruction of lymphatic drainage in the lower extremities from diabetic neuropathy impairing lymphatic vessel contractility

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased interstitial oncotic pressure from tissue glycosylation end-products trapping plasma proteins in the extravascular compartment

explanation.summary: This patient has nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia) from diabetic nephropathy with membranous changes. Massive urinary protein loss causes hypoalbuminemia, which decreases plasma oncotic pressure. Per Starling forces, reduced intracapillary oncotic pressure favors net filtration of fluid from capillaries into the interstitium, producing edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Albumin is the primary determinant of plasma oncotic pressure (approximately 70-80% of total colloid osmotic pressure). When nephrotic-range proteinuria causes serum albumin to fall to 2.1 g/dL, the oncotic pressure gradient favoring fluid reabsorption into capillaries is markedly reduced. This shifts the Starling equilibrium toward net filtration, causing fluid accumulation in the interstitial space and clinically manifest edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased glomerular hydrostatic pressure contributes to proteinuria but does not directly explain peripheral edema. Systemic capillary hydrostatic pressure in the lower extremities is not elevated by glomerular hypertension. The edema results from the downstream consequence of protein loss, not from the glomerular hemodynamic abnormality itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While diabetic microangiopathy does affect capillary structure, the dominant mechanism of edema in nephrotic syndrome is reduced plasma oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminemia, not generalized capillary leak. The proteinuria and severely low albumin point to the Starling force mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymphatic obstruction causes lymphedema, which is typically unilateral, non-pitting, and associated with skin thickening. Diabetic neuropathy affects peripheral nerves, not lymphatic vessels. This patient's bilateral pitting edema with nephrotic-range proteinuria is explained by hypoalbuminemia.
